title: Privacy Sandbox on Android
description: Privacy Sandbox on Android is Google's multi-year initiative to build privacy-preserving advertising technologies that replace cross-app identifiers. It introduces two key pillars: an **SDK Runtime** that isolates third-party SDKs in a sandboxed environment, and a set of privacy-preserving APIs (Topics, Protected Audience, Attribution Reporting) for ad relevance and measurement. Use it when building or integrating ad SDKs that must comply with evolving platform privacy requirements on Android 13+.

resources.title: Privacy Sandbox on Android | Android Developers
Introduction to the Privacy Sandbox on Android | Android Developers
android/privacy-sandbox-samples | GitHub
Privacy highlights from Google I/O 2024 | Privacy Sandbox Blog
Privacy Sandbox at Google I/O 2024 | Google Developers

title: Photo Picker
description: The Photo Picker is a system-provided UI that lets users share only the specific images or videos they select with your app, rather than granting broad access to their entire media library. It is available on Android 11 (API 30)+ via Google Play system updates and can be backported to Android 4.4 through Play Services. **Always prefer Photo Picker over broad storage permissions** for any feature that requires user-selected media, as it requires no permission declaration at all.

resources.title: Photo picker | App data and files | Android Developers
Permissionless is the future of Storage on Android | Android Developers Blog
Photo Picker Everywhere | Android Developers Blog
Cloud photos now available in the Android photo picker | Android Developers Blog
Now in Android #76 – Webkit, Compose Compiler, Photo Picker | Android Developers

title: Granular Media Permissions
description: Android 13 (API 33) replaced the broad `READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE` permission with three granular permissions: `READ_MEDIA_IMAGES`, `READ_MEDIA_VIDEO`, and `READ_MEDIA_AUDIO`. Apps declare only the media types they actually access, reducing unnecessary data exposure to users. Android 14 adds `READ_MEDIA_VISUAL_USER_SELECTED` for partial photo/video access. Adopt these when your app genuinely needs programmatic access to media files created by other apps and Photo Picker is not suitable.

resources.title: Access media files from shared storage | Android Developers
Behavior changes: Apps targeting Android 13 or higher | Android Developers
What's new in Android privacy and security | Google I/O 2023
I/O 2023: What's new in Android security and privacy | Google Security Blog
I/O 2024: What's new in Android security and privacy | Google Security Blog

title: Notification Permission
description: Android 13 (API 33) introduced `POST_NOTIFICATIONS`, a runtime permission that apps must request before they can display non-exempt notifications. New installs on Android 13+ start with notifications off; existing app updates may receive an automatic grant for eligible apps. Request the permission in context—tied to a feature the user is actively enabling—rather than at cold launch, to maximise grant rates and respect user intent.

resources.title: Notification runtime permission | Android Developers
Behavior changes: Apps targeting Android 13 or higher | Android Developers
What's new in Android privacy and security | Google I/O 2023
I/O 2023: What's new in Android security and privacy | Google Security Blog
I/O 2024: What's new in Android security and privacy | Google Security Blog

title: Scoped Storage
description: Scoped Storage, enforced from Android 11 (API 30) for apps targeting API 30+, restricts direct filesystem access to external storage. Apps can freely read and write their own app-specific directories (via `getExternalFilesDir()`) and contribute to shared media collections through MediaStore, but they cannot browse arbitrary paths on the device. This model protects user data from background file exfiltration and is the storage foundation all modern Android apps must be built on.

resources.title: Storage updates in Android 11 | Android Developers
Data and file storage overview | Android Developers
Preparing for scoped storage (Android Dev Summit '19) | Android Developers
Storage access with Android 11 | Android Developers
What's new in shared storage (Google I/O '19) | Android Developers

title: App Set ID
description: App Set ID is a privacy-friendly, resettable identifier provided by Google Play Services for correlating usage across apps owned by the same developer, without relying on persistent device identifiers like the Advertising ID. It is scoped either per-app or per-developer and is never shared across different developer accounts. Use it for analytics, fraud detection, and frequency capping scenarios where you need cross-app correlation within your own portfolio but must respect user privacy.

resources.title: Identify developer-owned apps | Identity | Android Developers
Best practices for unique identifiers | Identity | Android Developers
I/O 2023: What's new in Android security and privacy | Google Security Blog
What's new in Android privacy and security | Google I/O 2023
Privacy Sandbox on Android overview | Privacy Sandbox

title: Credential Manager / Passkeys
description: Credential Manager is the unified Jetpack API for authenticating users on Android, supporting **passkeys** (FIDO2/WebAuthn), Sign-in with Google, and traditional passwords through a single, consistent UI surface. Passkeys replace passwords with public-key cryptography bound to the device, making phishing attacks impossible. Use Credential Manager for any new authentication flow on Android 9+ as it replaces the deprecated Google Sign-In, One Tap, Smart Lock, and FIDO2 APIs.

resources.title: About Credential Manager | Identity | Android Developers
Passkeys – Android Developers Blog
How to reduce reliance on passwords in Android apps with passkey support | Google I/O 2023
Learn passkeys for simpler and safer sign-in | Android Developers
Passkeys | Google for Developers

title: Biometric API
description: The Jetpack Biometric library (`androidx.biometric`) provides a single API to authenticate users with fingerprint, face, or device credential (PIN/pattern/password) across all Android versions. It presents the platform-standard `BiometricPrompt` dialog and handles fallbacks automatically. Use it to gate access to sensitive in-app features or to unlock cryptographic keys stored in the Android Keystore, ensuring users are protected even if the device lacks specific biometric hardware.

resources.title: Show a biometric authentication dialog | Identity | Android Developers
Biometric | Jetpack | Android Developers
One Biometric API Over all Android | Android Developers Blog
Biometric Authentication on Android — Part 1 | Android Developers Medium
What's new in Android privacy and security | Google I/O 2023

title: Network Security Config
description: Network Security Config is an XML-based configuration file that lets you customise your app's TLS/SSL behaviour without writing code: pin certificates, trust custom CAs, restrict cleartext traffic, and configure debug-only overrides. From Android 9 (API 28) cleartext HTTP is blocked by default; this config is the recommended way to declare any necessary exceptions. Apply it to all apps that make network requests to ensure traffic is encrypted and certificate validation is strict.

resources.title: Network security configuration | Security | Android Developers
Improve your app's security | Security | Android Developers
Network Security Config - Android | YouTube
Network Security Config - Google Codelab - Android | YouTube
I/O 2024: What's new in Android security and privacy | Google Security Blog

title: Data Safety (Play Console)
description: The Data Safety section in Play Console is the mandatory disclosure form where developers declare what user data their app collects or shares, whether data is encrypted in transit, and whether users can request deletion. Google displays this information on your Play Store listing, and discrepancies between declared and actual behaviour can result in policy enforcement. Complete it accurately for every app before publishing and update it whenever your data-handling practices change.

resources.title: Declare your app's data use | Privacy | Android Developers
Provide information for Google Play's Data safety section – Play Console Help
Introducing the new Data safety section in Play Console | Android Developers
What's new in Android privacy and security | Google I/O 2023
I/O 2024: What's new in Android security and privacy | Google Security Blog

title: AICore
description: AICore is the Android system service introduced in Android 14 that manages the lifecycle of on-device AI foundation models, including downloading, updating, and serving Gemini Nano. It handles model weights, hardware acceleration selection (Google, MediaTek, Qualcomm NPUs), and input/output safety—so your app does not need to bundle large model files. Access AICore through the ML Kit GenAI APIs or the lower-level AICore developer preview APIs on supported Pixel and partner devices.

resources.title: Gemini Nano | AI | Android Developers
AICore Developer Preview program | ML Kit | Google for Developers
A walkthrough for Android's on-device GenAI solutions | Spotlight Week | Android Developers
Gemini Nano on Android: Building with on-device gen AI | Android Developers
Gemini Nano is now available on Android via experimental access | Android Developers Blog

title: Gemini Nano
description: Gemini Nano is Google's smallest Gemini model, optimised to run entirely on-device on supported Android smartphones. It executes locally with no network round-trip, providing privacy (prompts never leave the device), offline capability, and low latency. AICore manages the model and future updates transparently. Use Gemini Nano via ML Kit GenAI APIs for summarisation, proofreading, smart replies, and other NLU tasks on Pixel 9 series and newer Gemini-capable devices.

resources.title: Gemini Nano | AI | Android Developers
On-device GenAI APIs as part of ML Kit help you easily build with Gemini Nano | Android Developers Blog
Gemini Nano on Android: Building with on-device gen AI | Android Developers
A walkthrough for Android's on-device GenAI solutions | Spotlight Week | Android Developers
An introduction to privacy and safety for Gemini Nano | Android Developers Blog

title: ML Kit GenAI APIs
description: ML Kit GenAI APIs are high-level, task-oriented interfaces built on top of Gemini Nano via AICore, offering ready-made on-device AI tasks: **Summarization**, **Proofreading**, **Rewriting**, and **Image Description**. They abstract the complexity of prompt engineering and model management, and support both streaming and non-streaming response modes. Use them when you need on-device generative AI without the overhead of managing models directly, on Pixel 9 series and other AICore-enabled devices.

resources.title: ML Kit GenAI APIs | Android Developers
Overview of the ML Kit GenAI APIs | Google for Developers
A walkthrough for Android's on-device GenAI solutions | Spotlight Week | Android Developers
Gemini Nano on Android: Building with on-device gen AI | Android Developers
On-device GenAI APIs as part of ML Kit help you easily build with Gemini Nano | Android Developers Blog

title: ML Kit (Vision/Text/Translate)
description: ML Kit provides a suite of production-ready, on-device machine learning APIs for common mobile tasks: **Vision** (text recognition, barcode scanning, face detection, object detection, pose estimation), **Natural Language** (language identification, translation across 50+ languages, smart reply). Models run fully on-device, requiring no internet connection after optional model download. Integrate specific ML Kit APIs when your app needs reliable, privacy-safe ML inference without managing TFLite models manually.

resources.title: ML Kit | Google for Developers
Translate text with ML Kit on Android | Google for Developers
Easy on-device Machine Learning with MediaPipe | Google I/O 2023
googlesamples/mlkit | GitHub
Welcome to AI on Android Spotlight Week | Android Developers Blog

title: LiteRT (TFLite)
description: LiteRT (formerly TensorFlow Lite) is Google's cross-platform runtime for deploying custom ML models on-device across Android, iOS, desktop, and embedded systems. It supports models from TensorFlow, PyTorch, JAX, and Keras converted to the `.tflite` flatbuffer format, and accelerates inference via GPU, DSP, and NPU delegates. Use LiteRT when you need to run a custom-trained model (e.g., a fine-tuned image classifier or domain-specific NLP model) with maximum hardware acceleration on Android.

resources.title: LiteRT: High-Performance On-Device ML Framework | Google AI Edge
LiteRT for Android | Google AI Edge
google-ai-edge/LiteRT | GitHub
A walkthrough for Android's on-device GenAI solutions | Spotlight Week | Android Developers
LiteRT: The Universal Framework for On-Device AI | Google Developers Blog

title: MediaPipe Tasks
description: MediaPipe Tasks is the high-level API layer of the MediaPipe Solutions suite, providing pre-built, cross-platform task libraries for vision (object detection, image segmentation, hand/face/pose landmark detection), text (text classification, language detection), and audio (audio classification). Each task bundles a curated model with a clean API, handling pre/post-processing internally. Use MediaPipe Tasks when you need streaming, real-time ML on live camera or audio feeds without assembling a custom LiteRT pipeline.

resources.title: MediaPipe Tasks | Google AI Edge
Setup guide for Android | MediaPipe | Google AI Edge
Easy on-device Machine Learning with MediaPipe | Google I/O 2023
google-ai-edge/mediapipe | GitHub
Introducing MediaPipe Solutions for On-Device Machine Learning | Google Developers Blog

title: Firebase AI Logic
description: Firebase AI Logic is Firebase's client SDK and proxy layer for calling Google's Gemini API (both Gemini Developer API and Vertex AI Gemini API) directly from Android, iOS, Flutter, and web apps without a backend server. It integrates with **Firebase App Check** to protect your API keys from abuse, and supports hybrid inference that seamlessly falls back from on-device to cloud. Use it when you want fast, secure access to Gemini cloud models with Firebase's mobile-first security and configuration tooling.

resources.title: Gemini API using Firebase AI Logic | Firebase
Get started with the Gemini API using the Firebase AI Logic SDKs | Firebase
Gemini Nano on Android: Building with on-device gen AI | Android Developers
Google I/O 2024 recap: Making AI accessible and helpful for every developer | Google Developers Blog
Firebase AI Logic sessions | Google I/O 2024

title: Vertex AI in Firebase
description: Vertex AI in Firebase exposes Google Cloud's Vertex AI Gemini API through the Firebase mobile SDK (`firebase-vertexai`), bringing enterprise-grade scalability, data-privacy guarantees (prompts are never used for model training), and Vertex AI's model lineup directly into Android and other client apps. It is generally available and production-ready, backed by Google Cloud SLAs. Choose it over Firebase AI Logic when you need Vertex AI's advanced models, regions, and compliance guarantees for production workloads.

resources.title: Gemini API using Vertex AI in Firebase | Firebase

Firebase AI Logic sessions | Google I/O 2024

title: expect / actual
description: The `expect`/`actual` mechanism is Kotlin Multiplatform's compiler-enforced way to declare a platform-agnostic API in `commonMain` and supply platform-specific implementations in each target source set. An `expect` declaration (function, class, object, etc.) carries no implementation body; each platform provides its matching `actual` declaration. The compiler enforces that every `expect` has a corresponding `actual` for every configured target, making it the primary tool for wrapping platform-native APIs cleanly.

resources.title: Expected and actual declarations | Kotlin Multiplatform Documentation
"Expect Actual" Mechanism in Kotlin Multiplatform Explained | ProAndroidDev
KotlinConf 2024 full playlist | Kotlin by JetBrains
Kotlin Multiplatform Developer Roadmap | skydoves/kmp-developer-roadmap
Level Up with Kotlin Multiplatform | JetBrains Learning Journey

title: commonMain
description: `commonMain` is the default shared source set in a KMP project where all code that compiles to every configured target lives. Business logic, data models, use cases, and any multiplatform library calls go here. The Kotlin compiler merges `commonMain` with each platform source set during compilation, so declarations in `commonMain` are available everywhere. Keeping as much logic as possible in `commonMain` is the central goal of a KMP architecture—it maximises code reuse across Android, iOS, desktop, and web.

resources.title: The basics of Kotlin Multiplatform project structure | Kotlin Documentation
Create your Kotlin Multiplatform app | Kotlin Multiplatform Documentation
KotlinConf 2024 full playlist | Kotlin by JetBrains
Kotlin Multiplatform Developer Roadmap | skydoves/kmp-developer-roadmap
Level Up with Kotlin Multiplatform | JetBrains Learning Journey

title: androidMain
description: `androidMain` is the Android-specific source set in a KMP project where you provide `actual` implementations of `expect` declarations and write any code that depends on the Android SDK. Android Jetpack libraries, Context-dependent APIs, and Android-only dependencies all belong here. Code in `androidMain` is compiled only for the Android target, keeping the shared module free of Android-specific imports while still letting you access the full Android platform when needed.

resources.title: The basics of Kotlin Multiplatform project structure | Kotlin Documentation
Use platform-specific APIs | Kotlin Multiplatform Documentation
KotlinConf 2024 full playlist | Kotlin by JetBrains
Kotlin Multiplatform Developer Roadmap | skydoves/kmp-developer-roadmap
Google @ KotlinConf 2024: A Look Inside Multiplatform Development with KMP | Android Developers Blog

title: iosMain
description: `iosMain` is the iOS-specific source set that supplies `actual` implementations of `expect` declarations using Kotlin/Native's Swift/Objective-C interop layer. It compiles to a native iOS framework and has access to Apple's SDK through `kotlinx.cinterop` and the generated Objective-C bindings. Anything that must call CoreLocation, UIKit, or another Apple framework lives here. A properly structured `iosMain` is what enables your shared KMP library to produce a first-class native iOS binary.

resources.title: Adding iOS dependencies | Kotlin Multiplatform Documentation
Interoperability with Swift/Objective-C | Kotlin Documentation
Evolving Compose Multiplatform on iOS and Beyond | KotlinConf 2024
Kotlin Multiplatform Developer Roadmap | skydoves/kmp-developer-roadmap
Google @ KotlinConf 2024: A Look Inside Multiplatform Development with KMP | Android Developers Blog

title: Compose Multiplatform
description: Compose Multiplatform is JetBrains' framework for sharing **Jetpack Compose UI** across Android, iOS (stable since 1.8.0), desktop (Windows/macOS/Linux), and web (Wasm/Beta). It reuses the same composable functions, state management, and animations across platforms, dramatically reducing UI duplication in KMP projects. Use it when you want a single Compose codebase for your UI layer; for pure Android projects, standard Jetpack Compose remains the recommendation.

resources.title: Get started with Compose Multiplatform | Kotlin Multiplatform Documentation

Evolving Compose Multiplatform on iOS and Beyond | KotlinConf 2024
Kotlin Multiplatform Developer Roadmap | skydoves/kmp-developer-roadmap
Level Up with Kotlin Multiplatform | JetBrains Learning Journey

title: Ktor
description: Ktor is JetBrains' asynchronous HTTP client (and server) framework written in Kotlin, designed from the ground up for KMP. It runs on JVM, Android, iOS (Kotlin/Native), JS, and WASM using platform-appropriate engines (OkHttp on Android, Darwin on iOS, etc.) while sharing request configuration, serialisation, and plugin setup in `commonMain`. Use Ktor as the standard KMP networking layer whenever your shared module needs to make HTTP calls across platforms.

resources.title: Creating a cross-platform mobile application | Ktor Documentation
Ktor: Build Asynchronous Servers and Clients in Kotlin
Ktor for Networking in Kotlin Multiplatform Mobile projects | YouTube
ktorio/ktor | GitHub
Level Up with Kotlin Multiplatform | JetBrains Learning Journey

title: SQLDelight
description: SQLDelight generates fully type-safe Kotlin APIs from plain `.sq` SQL files, verified at compile time. It supports SQLite on Android, iOS, JVM, and JS through platform-specific driver implementations, making it the go-to SQL persistence library for KMP. SQL statements are written once in shared code and exposed as suspend-friendly Kotlin functions with full IDE support. Use SQLDelight when you need relational persistence in a KMP project and want compile-time SQL correctness guarantees.

resources.title: Overview - SQLDelight
sqldelight/sqldelight | GitHub
KotlinConf 2018 – A Multiplatform Delight by Jake Wharton and Alec Strong | Kotlin
SQLite/SQLDelight + Kotlin Multiplatform | YouTube
Level Up with Kotlin Multiplatform | JetBrains Learning Journey

title: kotlinx.coroutines
description: `kotlinx.coroutines` is the official Kotlin library for structured concurrency, providing `Flow`, `Channel`, coroutine scopes, dispatchers, and synchronisation primitives. It is fully multiplatform—supporting JVM, Android, iOS/Native, JS, and WASM—and is the standard async runtime for all KMP projects. In `commonMain` you use platform-agnostic dispatchers (`Dispatchers.Default`, `Dispatchers.IO` on JVM/Android) and `Flow` for reactive streams; platform modules provide native integration where needed.

resources.title: Concurrency and coroutines | Kotlin Multiplatform Documentation

KotlinConf 2024 full playlist | Kotlin by JetBrains

Level Up with Kotlin Multiplatform | JetBrains Learning Journey

title: kotlinx.serialization
description: `kotlinx.serialization` is Kotlin's official multiplatform serialisation framework. It is a **compiler plugin + runtime library** combination: you annotate classes with `@Serializable` and the compiler generates efficient serialisers at compile time with no reflection. It supports JSON, CBOR, ProtoBuf, and other formats, and runs on every KMP target. Use it in `commonMain` for parsing API responses (especially with Ktor), persisting data, or any format conversion across platforms.

resources.title: Serialization | Kotlin Documentation
Kotlin/kotlinx.serialization | GitHub
KotlinConf 2024 full playlist | Kotlin by JetBrains
Kotlin Multiplatform Developer Roadmap | skydoves/kmp-developer-roadmap
Level Up with Kotlin Multiplatform | JetBrains Learning Journey

title: Koin
description: Koin is a lightweight, pragmatic dependency injection framework for Kotlin and KMP that requires no code generation or reflection—modules are declared in a DSL and verified at compile time via the Koin Compiler Plugin. It supports Android, iOS, desktop, and Compose Multiplatform targets with platform-specific integration (e.g., `KoinAndroidContext`, `koinViewModel`). Use Koin when you need DI in a KMP project without the annotation-processing overhead of Dagger/Hilt, which do not run in `commonMain`.

resources.title: Kotlin Multiplatform Dependency Injection | Koin

Level Up with Kotlin Multiplatform | JetBrains Learning Journey

title: Decompose
description: Decompose is a KMP library for component-based architecture with built-in navigation and lifecycle management. It organises UI into a tree of `ComponentContext`-aware components, each with its own lifecycle, back-press handling, and state retention—all in shared Kotlin code. It supports pluggable UI renderers: Jetpack Compose, Compose Multiplatform, SwiftUI, and JS React. Use Decompose when you want to share not just business logic but also navigation logic and component lifecycle across all KMP targets.

title: Voyager
description: Voyager is a pragmatic navigation library purpose-built for Compose Multiplatform. It uses a `Screen` abstraction with a stack-based `Navigator`, and supports linear, tab, bottom-sheet, and nested navigation patterns out of the box. `ScreenModel`—its equivalent of ViewModel—integrates with Koin, Kodein, and Hilt for DI. Use Voyager when you want a straightforward, Compose-idiomatic navigation solution that works identically on Android, iOS, desktop, and web without platform-specific wiring.

resources.title: Voyager – A pragmatic navigation library for Compose Multiplatform

title: Coil 3
description: Coil 3 is a Kotlin-first image loading library that is now a full **Compose Multiplatform library**, supporting Android, iOS, JVM, JS, and WASM. It provides the familiar `AsyncImage` composable and handles memory/disk caching, request cancellation, and image downsampling across all targets. On KMP it uses Ktor instead of OkHttp for network fetching. Upgrade from Coil 2 (Maven group `io.coil-kt.coil3`) when adding iOS or desktop targets to your Compose Multiplatform project.

resources.title: Coil – Image loading for Android and Compose Multiplatform

title: Room KMP
description: Room 2.7+ brings the full Room database API—entities, DAOs, migrations, and `Flow`-based reactive queries—to Kotlin Multiplatform, adding iOS and JVM targets alongside Android. The KMP version uses the `androidx.sqlite` driver APIs (also KMP-compatible) and enforces `suspend` functions for DAO methods on non-Android targets. Room 3.0 (in development) further expands KMP support to JS and WASM. Use Room KMP when you want the familiar Room API with compile-time SQL verification in a shared KMP module.

title: cinterop
description: `cinterop` is the Kotlin/Native tool that generates Kotlin bindings for C and Objective-C libraries, enabling `iosMain` (and other Native source sets) to call Apple platform APIs and third-party native libraries. You provide a `.def` file describing the headers and library, and `cinterop` produces a Kotlin API you can call naturally. It underpins all Kotlin-to-Apple-SDK communication in KMP—every iOS framework call you make from Kotlin ultimately goes through cinterop-generated bindings.

resources.title: Interoperability with C | Kotlin Documentation

Kotlin Multiplatform Developer Roadmap | skydoves/kmp-developer-roadmap
Level Up with Kotlin Multiplatform | JetBrains Learning Journey

title: Swift Export
description: Swift Export is an experimental Kotlin/Native feature (available by default from Kotlin 2.2.20) that generates idiomatic Swift APIs from Kotlin Multiplatform code, replacing the Objective-C header bridge with native Swift types, generics, and async/await. Each Kotlin module is exported as a separate Swift module, preserving package structure. It is not yet production-ready (stable release targeted for 2026), but use it in early adoption projects that need first-class Swift interoperability without the impedance mismatch of Objective-C headers.
